summary: I served in the United States Army during World War II, specifically in the infantry branch. I was trained at Fort Benning and later assigned to the 2nd Philippine Scouts to train troops in the Philippines. My time in the Army was intense, especially during the war and my experience as a prisoner of war. I was proud to serve and later became involved with the Disabled American Veterans organization.

I finished my service with the Army's 4th Infantry Division. That was the unit I was part of when I entered active duty. It was a significant part of my military career, though much of my combat and training experience was tied to other assignments like the Philippines. The 4th Infantry Division was where I started and it shaped my early days in the Army.

At the time I joined the Army, I was living in Anniston, Alabama, though I wasn't originally from there. My family was actually in Oklahoma City. Anniston was where I was residing when I made the decision to enter service and start my military journey.

My folks were living in Oklahoma City when I joined the Army, even though I was living in Anniston, Alabama. Oklahoma City was my family’s home, and though I was away training and serving, that’s where my roots were. I kept in touch with them as best I could during the war.

I went to infantry school at Fort Benning, Georgia, for three months to learn how to be an officer. That was where I got my training as a lieutenant, and it was a crucial part of my preparation before being sent overseas. Fort Benning was the place that shaped my leadership skills before the war.

I served in World War II for three and a half years, mostly in the Philippines. My experience included training troops, fighting in battle, and surviving as a prisoner of war after the fall of Bataan. The war profoundly affected my life, and I often reflect on the hardships and camaraderie we shared during those years.

I was sent to the Philippines during World War II to train the 2nd Philippine Scouts. It was my first time traveling outside the U.S., and the journey there was exciting despite the challenges. The Philippines became a battleground where I saw combat, endured the fall of Bataan, and later survived as a prisoner of war under Japanese captivity.

While in the Philippines, I was under the command of General Douglas MacArthur. He was a key figure during the war, and being part of his theater of operations was significant. MacArthur's leadership was well known, and we were tasked with defending the islands as the war unfolded.

I was assigned to the 2nd Philippine Scouts when I arrived in the Philippines. They were trained troops, but many had never handled guns before, so my job was to train them quickly in infantry skills. We had very little ammunition and time before the war started, so it was a tough challenge preparing them for combat.

I fought in the Battle of Bataan, which ended with General King surrendering to the Japanese on April 9. We were starving and out of supplies, and the battle was brutal. Many of us were captured and forced to endure the horrific Bataan Death March afterward.

General Jonathan M. Wainwright was one of the senior commanders during the fall of the Philippines. While I mentioned General King who surrendered, Wainwright was also a key figure in the defense efforts. His leadership was part of the larger story of the Philippines campaign in which I served.

I survived the Bataan Death March, which was one of the worst atrocities of the war. After the surrender, thousands of us were forced to march under brutal conditions, with many dying along the way. It was a fanatical and horrific event, and I was fortunate to live through it. Congress later awarded me the Bronze Star for surviving that ordeal.

After the Death March, I was held as a prisoner of war in camps in Japan for three and a half years. We were kept in the mountains, starving and suffering, until the war ended in August 1945. The Japanese guards were brutal, but eventually, we were liberated in September when food was dropped by B-29 Superfortress planes.

The B-29 Superfortress planes played a crucial role in our survival as prisoners of war in Japan. They dropped food and juice by parachute to our camp in the mountains, which helped keep us alive until liberation. Their support was vital during those final months of captivity.

I was awarded the Bronze Star Medal for surviving the Bataan Death March. Congress recognized the suffering and endurance of those of us who made it through that ordeal. It was an honor to receive that medal, which symbolizes the hardships we endured and our resilience.

I am a member of the Disabled American Veterans organization, which has been instrumental in helping me and others with benefits and support. In 1982, Congress passed a law recognizing all prisoners of war as 100 percent disabled, and the DAV helped me get that status quickly. They continue to take care of veterans like me, providing essential services and advocacy.

After the war, my wife and I organized a reunion in Dallas with many couples from our Army days. We had about 62 couples come together to reconnect and share memories. Dallas became a place where we could celebrate our shared experiences and maintain lifelong friendships.
